howTo.name: How to use the cm to px converter
howTo.description: Instructions: cm to pixels at DPI.

contentBlocks.title: When do you need to convert cm to pixels?
contentBlocks.html:
In print design, dimensions are specified in centimeters or inches, but digital images are measured in pixels. The conversion depends on the DPI (dots per inch) resolution: pixels = cm × DPI / 2.54.

Standard print resolution is 300 DPI: 10 cm = 1,181 pixels, 21 cm (A4 width) = 2,480 pixels. Web resolution is typically 72 DPI: 10 cm = 283 pixels.

This converter is essential for preparing images for print, setting up document templates, and calculating the required image resolution for a specific print size.

All calculations run locally in your browser — nothing is sent to any server.

items.title: 1. Enter a value
items.description: Type a number in the input field. You can use a period or comma as the decimal separator.

items.title: 2. Read the result
items.description: The conversion result appears instantly in the field next to it – no clicking required.

items.title: 3. Copy or reverse
items.description: Click Copy result or use the Reverse button to convert in the opposite direction.

items.title: Print preparation for brochures and posters
items.description: Professional print requires 300 DPI. An A4 brochure (21 × 29.7 cm) at 300 DPI needs a 2,480 × 3,508px canvas. An A3 poster (29.7 × 42 cm) requires 3,508 × 4,961px. Getting this wrong and delivering a 72 DPI file results in a blurry, unprintable product — this converter confirms the correct canvas size before you start designing.

items.title: Web banner and display advertising
items.description: Web banners are often specified in centimeters by clients but must be delivered in pixels. A standard leaderboard banner at 72.8 × 9 cm at 96 DPI screen resolution equals 756 × 94px. For retina-ready banners, double the DPI to 192 DPI so the banner stays sharp on high-DPI displays.

items.title: CSS @media print stylesheets
items.description: CSS @media print allows using cm units directly — width: 21cm renders correctly when printed. However, when preparing background images or raster graphics for print stylesheets, you still need to convert cm to px at the appropriate DPI to supply a high-resolution image that prints sharply.

items.title: Photo books and large format printing
items.description: Photo book printing services like Shutterfly and Blurb specify page dimensions in centimeters and require images at 300 DPI. A 20 × 20 cm square page requires a 2,362 × 2,362px image. For large-format prints (banners, canvas), 150 DPI is usually sufficient — a 60 × 90 cm banner at 150 DPI = 3,543 × 5,315px.

items.title: Complete privacy
items.description: All calculations run locally in your browser. No data is sent to any server.

items.title: Real-time results
items.description: The result updates instantly as you type – no need to click any button.

items.title: Bidirectional conversion
items.description: The Reverse button lets you convert in both directions with one click.

items.title: Reference table
items.description: A ready-made table with commonly converted values and contextual descriptions.

contentBlocks.title: How does cm to pixel conversion work?
contentBlocks.html:
The formula is: pixels = cm × DPI / 2.54. DPI means dots per inch, and 1 inch = 2.54 cm.

At 300 DPI: 1 cm = 118.11 pixels. At 72 DPI: 1 cm = 28.35 pixels.

Higher DPI = more pixels per centimeter = sharper print. 300 DPI is the standard for professional print quality.

contentBlocks.title: Practical tips
contentBlocks.html:
• 300 DPI (print quality): A4 = 2,480 × 3,508 px, A3 = 3,508 × 4,961 px.
  • 150 DPI (large format): A2 poster = 2,480 × 3,508 px.
  • 72 DPI (screen): 10 cm = 283 px, 20 cm = 567 px.
  • 12 MP camera (4,000 × 3,000 px) prints up to 33.9 × 25.4 cm at 300 DPI.

items.question: How many pixels is 1 cm at 300 DPI?
items.answer: 1 cm at 300 DPI = 118.11 pixels. The formula is: pixels = cm × DPI / 2.54. So 1 cm × 300 / 2.54 = 118.11px. At 96 DPI (screen): 1 cm = 37.8px. At 72 DPI (screen legacy): 1 cm = 28.35px.

items.question: What DPI should I use for different print types?
items.answer: 300 DPI: standard for professional print — brochures, business cards, photo prints, magazines. 150 DPI: acceptable for large-format printing — posters, banners, canvas prints viewed from a distance. 72–96 DPI: screen only — web graphics, digital presentations, on-screen PDFs.

items.question: How big can I print a 12 megapixel photo?
items.answer: A 12 MP camera produces roughly 4,000 × 3,000 pixels. At 300 DPI: 4000/300 × 2.54 = 33.9 × 25.4 cm (about 13×10 inches). At 150 DPI: 67.7 × 50.8 cm. Beyond these sizes, visible pixelation may appear at normal viewing distance.

items.question: What resolution is A4 at 300 DPI?
items.answer: A4 paper (21 × 29.7 cm) at 300 DPI = 2,480 × 3,508 pixels. This is the standard canvas size requested by most commercial print shops for full-bleed A4 documents. Add 3mm bleed on each side: 21.6 × 30.3 cm = 2,551 × 3,579px.

items.question: What is 96 DPI and why is it the screen standard?
items.answer: 96 DPI became the Windows screen standard because it gives a comfortable text size on early monitors. CSS defines 1 inch = 96 CSS pixels, so 1 cm = 96/2.54 = 37.795 CSS pixels. This is independent of the physical screen DPI, which on modern Retina displays can be 200+ PPI.

items.question: How do I set document size correctly in Photoshop for print?
items.answer: In Photoshop: File > New > choose Centimeters as the unit, enter your dimensions (e.g. 21 × 29.7 cm), set resolution to 300 pixels/inch. The pixel dimensions update automatically. For bleed, add 3mm to each side: enter 21.6 × 30.3 cm instead.

items.question: Does CSS use cm as a valid unit?
items.answer: Yes. CSS accepts cm as a unit — width: 10cm is valid. The browser converts it using the standard: 1cm = 37.795px at 96 DPI. This is only useful for print stylesheets (@media print) because screen pixels do not correspond to physical centimeters on screen.

items.question: Are calculations done locally?
items.answer: Yes. All calculations run in your browser. No data is sent to any server.
